sections.text: This Act may be cited as the Legal Aid Act, 2021, and shall come into operation on the date appointed by the Minister by statutory instrument.
This Act may be cited as the Legal Aid Act, 2021, and shall come into operation on the date appointed by the Minister by statutory instrument.

In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires— " alternative dispute resolution " includes mechanisms such as mediation, conciliation, negotiation and arbitration aimed at preventing, settling or resolving a dispute; " associate " has the meaning assigned to the word in the Anti-Corruption Act, 2012; [Act No. 3 of 2012 ] " Authority " means the Technical Education, Vocational and Entrepreneurship Training Authority established under the Technical Education, Vocational and Entrepreneurship Training Act, 1998; [Act No. 13 of 1998 ] " Board " means the Board of the Legal Aid Board constituted under section 8 ; " Chairperson " means the person appointed as the Chairperson of the Board under section 8 ; " civil society organisation " means a registered non-state legal entity not established or operated for profit; " court " has the meaning assigned to the word in the Constitution; [ Cap. 1 ] " Director " means the person appointed as Director of the Legal Aid Board under section 10 ; "

The Legal Aid Board established under the repealed Act shall continue to exist as if established under this Act. The Legal Aid Board is a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal, capable of suing and of being sued in its corporate name, and with powers, subject to the provisions of this Act, to do an act or thing that a body corporate may by law do or perform, as if established under this Act. The First Schedule applies to the Legal Aid Board .
The Legal Aid Board established under the repealed Act shall continue to exist as if established under this Act.

The Legal Aid Board is a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal, capable of suing and of being sued in its corporate name, and with powers, subject to the provisions of this Act, to do an act or thing that a body corporate may by law do or perform, as if established under this Act.
The First Schedule applies to the Legal Aid Board .

The functions of the Legal Aid Board are to— administer a comprehensive legal aid system in the Republic that is accessible, effective, impartial and sustainable; provide legal aid in civil and criminal matters; publish and disseminate information relating to legal aid ; coordinate the provision of legal aid by State and non state legal aid providers; regulate, oversee and monitor the provision of legal aid based on a quality assurance framework and standards; register a civil society organisation and higher education institution law clinic that intends to provide legal aid ; undertake regular assessment of legal aid provided by a registered higher education institution law clinic ; register practitioners, legal assistants and paralegals; manage and disburse funds from the Fund under this Act; mobilise financial resources; undertake research on aspects of legal aid ; facilitate the provision of legal aid to persons granted legal aid under this Act; and issue guidelines on the applicati

Legal aid consists of— legal education ; legal information ; legal advice ; legal assistance ; or legal representation . Unless otherwise provided under this Act— the provision of legal services by way of legal aid shall not affect the relationship between or the rights of a practitioner and client or any privilege arising out of that relationship; and the rights conferred by this Act on a legally aided person shall not affect the rights or liabilities of any other party to any matter or proceedings.
Legal aid consists of— legal education ; legal information ; legal advice ; legal assistance ; or legal representation .

Unless otherwise provided under this Act— the provision of legal services by way of legal aid shall not affect the relationship between or the rights of a practitioner and client or any privilege arising out of that relationship; and the rights conferred by this Act on a legally aided person shall not affect the rights or liabilities of any other party to any matter or proceedings.

There is constituted a Board of the Legal Aid Board . The Board consists of the following part-time members appointed by the Minister: a person qualified to be a judge of the High Court, who shall be the Chairperson ; a representative of the Ministry responsible for— justice; home affairs; finance; community development and social services; labour; and gender; a representative of the Law Association of Zambia; a representative of a civil society organisation providing legal aid ; a representative of a school of law of a higher education institution providing legal aid ; and Director , as ex officio . The members under subsection (2) shall be nominated by their respective ministries, organisations or institutions for appointment by the Minister. The Vice- Chairperson shall be elected by the members of the Board from among themselves. A person shall not be appointed as a member of the Board if that person— is an undischarged bankrupt; is convicted of an offence involving fraud or dishone
There is constituted a Board of the Legal Aid Board .

The Board consists of the following part-time members appointed by the Minister: a person qualified to be a judge of the High Court, who shall be the Chairperson ; a representative of the Ministry responsible for— justice; home affairs; finance; community development and social services; labour; and gender; a representative of the Law Association of Zambia; a representative of a civil society organisation providing legal aid ; a representative of a school of law of a higher education institution providing legal aid ; and Director , as ex officio .
The members under subsection (2) shall be nominated by their respective ministries, organisations or institutions for appointment by the Minister.
The Vice- Chairperson shall be elected by the members of the Board from among themselves.

A person shall not be appointed as a member of the Board if that person— is an undischarged bankrupt; is convicted of an offence involving fraud or dishonesty; is legally disqualified from performing the functions of a member; or is convicted of an offence under any other written law and sentenced to a term of imprisonment of not less than six months, without the option of a fine.

The Board is the governing body of the Legal Aid Board and shall carry out the functions under this Act. Despite the generality of subsection (1), the functions of the Board are to— promote effective corporate governance of the Legal Aid Board ; approve the annual budget estimates and financial statements of the Legal Aid Board ; advise the Minister on policies relating to the provision of legal aid and implement Government policies relating to the same; approve the annual work plan and activity reports of the Legal Aid Board ; and formulate the policies, programmes and strategies of the Legal Aid Board . Despite subsection (1), the Board may, by direction in writing and subject to conditions that the Board considers necessary, delegate to any member, committee or the Director any of its functions under this Act.
The Board is the governing body of the Legal Aid Board and shall carry out the functions under this Act.

Despite the generality of subsection (1), the functions of the Board are to— promote effective corporate governance of the Legal Aid Board ; approve the annual budget estimates and financial statements of the Legal Aid Board ; advise the Minister on policies relating to the provision of legal aid and implement Government policies relating to the same; approve the annual work plan and activity reports of the Legal Aid Board ; and formulate the policies, programmes and strategies of the Legal Aid Board .

Despite subsection (1), the Board may, by direction in writing and subject to conditions that the Board considers necessary, delegate to any member, committee or the Director any of its functions under this Act.

The Board shall appoint a Director who— is qualified to be appointed as judge of the High Court; is the chief executive officer of the Legal Aid Board ; is responsible for the day-to-day administration of the Legal Aid Board ; and shall perform corporate secretarial duties for the Board . The Director shall attend the meetings of the Board and any committee of the Board and may address those meetings, but shall have no vote. The Board shall, appoint a Chief Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistants and other staff of the Legal Aid Board that the Legal Aid Board considers necessary for the performance of the Board ’s functions under the Act. The Chief Legal Aid Counsel shall, under the direction of the Director — perform duties determined by the Board ; and discharge the functions of the Director if the Director is absent or is for any other reason unable to discharge the functions of the Director ’s office. The Emoluments Commission shall, on the recommendation of the

The Board shall appoint a Director who— is qualified to be appointed as judge of the High Court; is the chief executive officer of the Legal Aid Board ; is responsible for the day-to-day administration of the Legal Aid Board ; and shall perform corporate secretarial duties for the Board .
The Director shall attend the meetings of the Board and any committee of the Board and may address those meetings, but shall have no vote.

The Board shall, appoint a Chief Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistants and other staff of the Legal Aid Board that the Legal Aid Board considers necessary for the performance of the Board ’s functions under the Act.

The Chief Legal Aid Counsel shall, under the direction of the Director — perform duties determined by the Board ; and discharge the functions of the Director if the Director is absent or is for any other reason unable to discharge the functions of the Director ’s office.

The Emoluments Commission shall, on the recommendation of the Board , determine the emoluments of the Director , Chief Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistants and other staff of the Legal Aid Board .

The Board shall determine the conditions of service, other than the emoluments, of the Director , Chief Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistants and other staff of the Legal Aid Board .

A Legal Aid Assistant is, in any matter in which legal aid is provided, entitled to appear for and represent a legally aided person in any— criminal proceedings before a subordinate court ; civil proceedings before a subordinate court and the Lands Tribunal ; proceedings in chambers in the High Court before the Registrar , the Deputy Registrar or a Judge; and proceedings for Alternative Dispute Resolution, except arbitration. The right of audience conferred on Legal Aid Assistants by this section is in addition to, and not a derogation of, any other written law relating to the right of audience of a person who is not a practitioner .

A Legal Aid Assistant is, in any matter in which legal aid is provided, entitled to appear for and represent a legally aided person in any— criminal proceedings before a subordinate court ; civil proceedings before a subordinate court and the Lands Tribunal ; proceedings in chambers in the High Court before the Registrar , the Deputy Registrar or a Judge; and proceedings for Alternative Dispute Resolution, except arbitration.

The right of audience conferred on Legal Aid Assistants by this section is in addition to, and not a derogation of, any other written law relating to the right of audience of a person who is not a practitioner .

Subject to section 13 , the costs of legal services provided by a registered practitioner under this Act shall include sums on account of— the remuneration payable to the registered practitioner assigned to provide legal aid ; and expenses incurred by that registered practitioner while representing a legally aided person and that are properly attributable to time spent or work done by that practitioner in representing that person. The costs to be paid under subsection (1), shall not include any sum relating to allowances paid to a witness attending to give evidence in the proceedings for the purposes of which legal aid is granted to any person in any case where those allowances are payable under any other written law.

Subject to section 13 , the costs of legal services provided by a registered practitioner under this Act shall include sums on account of— the remuneration payable to the registered practitioner assigned to provide legal aid ; and expenses incurred by that registered practitioner while representing a legally aided person and that are properly attributable to time spent or work done by that practitioner in representing that person.

The costs to be paid under subsection (1), shall not include any sum relating to allowances paid to a witness attending to give evidence in the proceedings for the purposes of which legal aid is granted to any person in any case where those allowances are payable under any other written law.

A registered legal assistant shall provide legal aid , except for legal representation and arbitration. A registered legal assistant shall be supervised by a practitioner based on guidelines issued by the Board .
A registered legal assistant shall provide legal aid , except for legal representation and arbitration.
A registered legal assistant shall be supervised by a practitioner based on guidelines issued by the Board .

A registered paralegal shall provide legal services , that the Board may determine. A registered paralegal shall be supervised by a practitioner , a legal assistant or any other person that the Board may determine.
A registered paralegal shall provide legal services , that the Board may determine.
A registered paralegal shall be supervised by a practitioner , a legal assistant or any other person that the Board may determine.

A person, other than a practitioner , shall not offer a legal aid service, be employed or practice as a legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid serivice provider unless that person is registered to do so under this Act. A person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.

A person, other than a practitioner , shall not offer a legal aid service, be employed or practice as a legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid serivice provider unless that person is registered to do so under this Act.

A person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.

The Director may, within fourteen days of receipt of an application under sections 15 , 18 , 21 and 22 grant or reject the application. The Director shall, on being satisfied that the applicant meets the prescribed requirements, grant the application under sections 15 , 18 , 21 and 22 and shall, within fourteen days of that grant register the applicant, in a prescribed manner and form. The Director shall issue an applicant with a certificate of registration in the prescribed form. The Director shall, where it rejects an application under sections (1), inform the applicant and give reasons for the decision.
The Director may, within fourteen days of receipt of an application under sections 15 , 18 , 21 and 22 grant or reject the application.

The Director shall, on being satisfied that the applicant meets the prescribed requirements, grant the application under sections 15 , 18 , 21 and 22 and shall, within fourteen days of that grant register the applicant, in a prescribed manner and form.
The Director shall issue an applicant with a certificate of registration in the prescribed form.
The Director shall, where it rejects an application under sections (1), inform the applicant and give reasons for the decision.

Subject to this Act, the Director may suspend or cancel the certificate of registration of a— practitioner where that practitioner — obtained the certificate of registration through fraud, misrepresentation or concealment of a material fact; is found guilty of professional misconduct under the Legal Practitioner’s Act; [ Cap. 30 ] is legally disqualified ; or is convicted of an offence under this Act or any other written law and sentenced to imprisonment for a period exceeding six months without the option of a fine; legal assistant or paralegal , where that legal assistant or paralegal — is convicted of an offence under this Act or any other written law and sentenced to imprisonment for a period exceeding six months without the option of a fine; or legal aid service provider , where that legal aid service provider — fails to comply with a term or condition of the certificate of registration; enters into receivership or liquidation or takes up an action for voluntary winding up or diss

Subject to this Act, the Director may suspend or cancel the certificate of registration of a— practitioner where that practitioner — obtained the certificate of registration through fraud, misrepresentation or concealment of a material fact; is found guilty of professional misconduct under the Legal Practitioner’s Act; [ Cap. 30 ] is legally disqualified ; or is convicted of an offence under this Act or any other written law and sentenced to imprisonment for a period exceeding six months without the option of a fine; legal assistant or paralegal , where that legal assistant or paralegal — is convicted of an offence under this Act or any other written law and sentenced to imprisonment for a period exceeding six months without the option of a fine; or legal aid service provider , where that legal aid service provider — fails to comply with a term or condition of the certificate of registration; enters into receivership or liquidation or takes up an action for voluntary winding up or diss

The Director shall, before suspending or cancelling the certificate of registration under subsection (1), give the practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider , an opportunity to be heard.

The Director may, before cancelling the certificate of registration under subsection (1), suspend the certificate of registration of a practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider , for a specified period and on terms and conditions that the Director may determine.

Where the Director cancels the certificate of registration under this section, the name of the practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider shall be removed from the register and shall not be restored, except on conditions that may be prescribed.
A person whose certificate of registration is cancelled shall, within seven days of being notified of the cancellation, surrender the certificate of registration to the Director .

A holder of a certificate of registration shall renew that certificate annually in the prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee. A certificate of registration that is not renewed in accordance with subsection (1) is void.
A holder of a certificate of registration shall renew that certificate annually in the prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee.
A certificate of registration that is not renewed in accordance with subsection (1) is void.

A person whose certificate of registration is destroyed or lost may apply to the Director for a duplicate certificate in the prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee. The Director may, within fourteen days of receipt of an application under subsection (1), issue a duplicate certificate of registration to the applicant.
A person whose certificate of registration is destroyed or lost may apply to the Director for a duplicate certificate in the prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee.
The Director may, within fourteen days of receipt of an application under subsection (1), issue a duplicate certificate of registration to the applicant.

A person shall not— make or cause to be made, an unauthorised entry, alteration or erasure on a register, certificate of registration or on a certified copy of a register or certificate of registration; procure or attempt to procure registration under this Act by fraud, misrepresentation or the concealment of a material fact; impersonate a registered practitioner or use the title or designation of a registered legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider while not registered as a legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider under this Act; or forge a certificate of registration or other document issued under this Act. A person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.

A person shall not— make or cause to be made, an unauthorised entry, alteration or erasure on a register, certificate of registration or on a certified copy of a register or certificate of registration; procure or attempt to procure registration under this Act by fraud, misrepresentation or the concealment of a material fact; impersonate a registered practitioner or use the title or designation of a registered legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider while not registered as a legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider under this Act; or forge a certificate of registration or other document issued under this Act.

A person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.

Subject to sections 43 , 44 and 45 a person who intends to access legal aid shall apply to the Director in a prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee. A person who intends to apply for legal aid on behalf of another shall apply to the Director in a prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee.
Subject to sections 43 , 44 and 45 a person who intends to access legal aid shall apply to the Director in a prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee.
A person who intends to apply for legal aid on behalf of another shall apply to the Director in a prescribed manner and form on payment of a prescribed fee.

The Director shall, within thirty days of receipt of an application under section 34 , grant or reject the application and inform the applicant of the decision. The Director may grant legal aid to any applicant in criminal or civil matter who in the opinion of the Director — has insufficient means to enable the applicant to pay for legal services ; and having regard to the circumstances of the case or matter, it is in the interest of justice that the applicant should be provided with legal aid in that case or matter. Despite subsection (2), the Director shall not grant legal aid for the purpose of civil proceedings in any court unless the Director is satisfied that an applicant has reasonable grounds for taking, defending or being a party to these proceedings. The Director may at any time exercise discretion to invite any person to apply for legal aid if it appears to the Director that such person may be— eligible for legal aid under this section; and ignorant of that person’s right to
The Director shall, within thirty days of receipt of an application under section 34 , grant or reject the application and inform the applicant of the decision.

The Director may grant legal aid to any applicant in criminal or civil matter who in the opinion of the Director — has insufficient means to enable the applicant to pay for legal services ; and having regard to the circumstances of the case or matter, it is in the interest of justice that the applicant should be provided with legal aid in that case or matter.

Despite subsection (2), the Director shall not grant legal aid for the purpose of civil proceedings in any court unless the Director is satisfied that an applicant has reasonable grounds for taking, defending or being a party to these proceedings.

The Director may at any time exercise discretion to invite any person to apply for legal aid if it appears to the Director that such person may be— eligible for legal aid under this section; and ignorant of that person’s right to apply for legal aid .
For the purposes of this section—

Subject to this Act, a person in respect of whom a legal aid certificate has been granted shall be entitled to legal aid in the matter to which the legal aid certificate relates and to be provided either by a— Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistant or paralegal of the Legal Aid Board ; or practitioner .

The Director shall, where the Director rejects an application for a legal aid certificate— notify the applicant in writing stating the reasons for the decision; and inform the applicant of the applicant’s right to appeal to the Board . Where the Director refuses to grant a legal aid certificate under subsection (1), the applicant may appeal to the Board within thirty days of the Director ’s decision. The Board may, in determining an appeal under subsection (2)— set aside the decision of the Director rejecting the application for legal aid if the Board is satisfied that grounds exist which qualify or entitle the person to be granted legal aid ; or dismiss the appeal. The Board shall, where the Board dismisses an appeal under subsection (3)— record, in writing, the reasons, for the dismissal; and inform the applicant of the reasons for dismissal.

The Director shall, where the Director rejects an application for a legal aid certificate— notify the applicant in writing stating the reasons for the decision; and inform the applicant of the applicant’s right to appeal to the Board .
Where the Director refuses to grant a legal aid certificate under subsection (1), the applicant may appeal to the Board within thirty days of the Director ’s decision.

The Board may, in determining an appeal under subsection (2)— set aside the decision of the Director rejecting the application for legal aid if the Board is satisfied that grounds exist which qualify or entitle the person to be granted legal aid ; or dismiss the appeal.
The Board shall, where the Board dismisses an appeal under subsection (3)— record, in writing, the reasons, for the dismissal; and inform the applicant of the reasons for dismissal.

Subject to subsection (2), the Director may, at any time for any reason which the Director considers to be sufficient, terminate legal aid granted under this Act in any criminal or civil matter. The Director shall not terminate legal aid granted under sections 43 , 44 , 45 , 46 and 47 without leave of the court. The Director shall, where the Director terminates legal aid granted under subsection (1)— record, in writing, the reasons for that termination; and inform the legally aided person of that person’s right to appeal to the Board . Where the Director terminates legal aid granted under this Act, the legally aided person may appeal to the Board within thirty days of termination. The Board may, in determining an appeal under subsection (3)— set aside the decision of the Director terminating legal aid granted if the Board is satisfied that grounds exist which qualify or entitle the person to continue to be granted legal aid ; or dismiss the appeal. Where the Board dismisses an appeal u
Subject to subsection (2), the Director may, at any time for any reason which the Director considers to be sufficient, terminate legal aid granted under this Act in any criminal or civil matter.
The Director shall not terminate legal aid granted under sections 43 , 44 , 45 , 46 and 47 without leave of the court.

The Director shall, where the Director terminates legal aid granted under subsection (1)— record, in writing, the reasons for that termination; and inform the legally aided person of that person’s right to appeal to the Board .
Where the Director terminates legal aid granted under this Act, the legally aided person may appeal to the Board within thirty days of termination.

The Board may, in determining an appeal under subsection (3)— set aside the decision of the Director terminating legal aid granted if the Board is satisfied that grounds exist which qualify or entitle the person to continue to be granted legal aid ; or dismiss the appeal.
Where the Board dismisses an appeal under subsection (5), the Board shall — record, in writing, the reasons, for that dismissal; and inform the legally aided person of the reasons for dismissal.

The Fund established under the repealed Act shall continue to exist as if established under this Act, for purposes of providing legal services to persons granted legal aid in accordance with this Act. The Fund consists of monies that may— be appropriated by Parliament for the purposes of the Fund ; be paid to the Fund by way of fees, legal aid contributions or costs; and vest in, or accrue to, the Fund . The Board may— accept moneys by way of grants or donations from any source in Zambia, and subject to the approval of the Minister responsible for Finance, from any source outside the Republic; or raise by way of loans or otherwise, the monies that it may require for the performance of the Legal Aid Board ’s functions; and charge and collect fees provided by the Legal Aid Board .

The Fund established under the repealed Act shall continue to exist as if established under this Act, for purposes of providing legal services to persons granted legal aid in accordance with this Act.

The Fund consists of monies that may— be appropriated by Parliament for the purposes of the Fund ; be paid to the Fund by way of fees, legal aid contributions or costs; and vest in, or accrue to, the Fund .

The Board may— accept moneys by way of grants or donations from any source in Zambia, and subject to the approval of the Minister responsible for Finance, from any source outside the Republic; or raise by way of loans or otherwise, the monies that it may require for the performance of the Legal Aid Board ’s functions; and charge and collect fees provided by the Legal Aid Board .

The Board may apply the moneys of the Fund for— purposes of providing legal services to persons granted legal aid in accordance with this Act; the remuneration of practitioners under section 13 ; and other expenses incurred by a practitioner while representing a legally aided person under this Act except for a practitioner providing legal aid under the Legal Practitioners Act. [ Cap. 30 ]

The Board is responsible for the administration of the Fund . The Minister shall, by statutory instrument, provide for prudent controls of the Fund relating to— fiscal controls and accounting procedures governing the Fund ; reporting procedures for matter relating to the Fund ; and investment of the monies of the Fund . The Board shall cause to be kept proper books of account and other records relating to the accounts of the Fund . The Fund shall be audited annually by the Auditor-General or an auditor appointed by the Auditor-General. The auditor’s fees shall be paid by the Board .
The Board is responsible for the administration of the Fund .

The Minister shall, by statutory instrument, provide for prudent controls of the Fund relating to— fiscal controls and accounting procedures governing the Fund ; reporting procedures for matter relating to the Fund ; and investment of the monies of the Fund .
The Board shall cause to be kept proper books of account and other records relating to the accounts of the Fund .
The Fund shall be audited annually by the Auditor-General or an auditor appointed by the Auditor-General.
The auditor’s fees shall be paid by the Board .

A person charged with an offence before a court may apply to the court for legal aid and the court may grant a legal aid certificate under this Act, if the court considers that— the accused has insufficient means to enable the accused to pay for legal services ; having regard to the circumstances of the case, it is desirable in the interests of justice that the accused be provided with legal aid . A person who is convicted of, or sentenced for, an offence by a court and intends to appeal against that conviction or sentence or against any judgement or order affecting that person which was made in any criminal case may apply for legal aid to the court that convicted or sentenced that person. A person who becomes a respondent in an appeal in a criminal cause or matter before any court may apply for legal aid to that court . The court shall, where a court rejects an application for the grant of legal aid under subsections (1), (2) or (3)— record, in writing, the reasons for the rejection;

A person charged with an offence before a court may apply to the court for legal aid and the court may grant a legal aid certificate under this Act, if the court considers that— the accused has insufficient means to enable the accused to pay for legal services ; having regard to the circumstances of the case, it is desirable in the interests of justice that the accused be provided with legal aid .

A person who is convicted of, or sentenced for, an offence by a court and intends to appeal against that conviction or sentence or against any judgement or order affecting that person which was made in any criminal case may apply for legal aid to the court that convicted or sentenced that person.
A person who becomes a respondent in an appeal in a criminal cause or matter before any court may apply for legal aid to that court .

The court shall, where a court rejects an application for the grant of legal aid under subsections (1), (2) or (3)— record, in writing, the reasons for the rejection; and inform the accused or respondent of the accused or respondent’s right to appeal to a superior court .

A court may, in determining an appeal under subsection (4)— set aside the decision of a lower court refusing the application for legal aid if the superior court is satisfied that grounds exist which qualify or entitle the accused or respondent to be granted legal aid and may cause the issuance of a legal aid certificate; or dismiss the appeal.

The court shall, where a court dismisses an appeal under subsection (5)— record, in writing, the reasons, for that dismissal; and inform the accused or respondent of the accused or respondent’s right to appeal to an appropriate superior court .
Where a court dismisses an appeal under subsection (6), the accused or respondent may appeal to the appropriate superior court .
A court shall on the grant of legal aid under this section notify the Director .

The Director shall on receipt of the notice under subsection (8), issue a legal aid certificate and assign to the person granted legal aid for purposes of the criminal proceedings to which the certificate relates— a Legal Aid Counsel or Legal Aid Assistant; or a registered practitioner ;

Despite section 43 , a court shall grant a legal aid certificate in the prescribed manner and form where— a person is— charged with a specified offence ; or charged with an offence other than a specified offence and the court before which the person appears considers that, having regard to the circumstances of the case, it is desirable in the interests of justice that the person be granted legal aid ; the case is not the subject of a preliminary inquiry; and the court before which the person appears considers that the person has insufficient means to enable that person to pay for legal services . A court shall grant a legal aid certificate in a prescribed form if during preliminary inquiry held under the Criminal Procedure Code the court considers that— having regard to the circumstances of the case, it is necessary in the interests of justice that the accused be represented by a practitioner at that inquiry; and the accused has insufficient means to enable the accused to pay for legal

Despite section 43 , a court shall grant a legal aid certificate in the prescribed manner and form where— a person is— charged with a specified offence ; or charged with an offence other than a specified offence and the court before which the person appears considers that, having regard to the circumstances of the case, it is desirable in the interests of justice that the person be granted legal aid ; the case is not the subject of a preliminary inquiry; and the court before which the person appears considers that the person has insufficient means to enable that person to pay for legal services .

A court shall grant a legal aid certificate in a prescribed form if during preliminary inquiry held under the Criminal Procedure Code the court considers that— having regard to the circumstances of the case, it is necessary in the interests of justice that the accused be represented by a practitioner at that inquiry; and the accused has insufficient means to enable the accused to pay for legal services .

Whenever a court commits a person for trial before the High Court and the court considers that the accused has insufficient means to enable the accused to engage a practitioner to represent the accused, the committing court shall cause the issuance of a legal aid certificate in the prescribed form.
Despite section 43 , where a juvenile is brought before a court and is unrepresented, the court shall grant a legal aid certificate in a prescribed manner and form.
A court shall on the grant of legal aid under this section notify the Director .

The Director shall, on receipt of a notice under subsection (5) assign to the person granted legal aid for purposes of the criminal proceedings to which the certificate relates— a Legal Aid Counsel or Legal Aid Assistant; or a registered practitioner .
The Minister shall, by statutory instrument, prescribe for the purposes of this Part, certain class of offences as specified offences.

A court may at any time grant legal aid to a person who is a party in any civil proceedings, whether at first instance or on appeal, in which the State is also a party if the court considers that— the person satisfies the conditions under which legal aid may be granted to that person under this Act; and it is in the interests of justice that the person be represented by a practitioner , other than the Director . The powers of a court under subsection (1) shall be exercisable whether or not legal aid is applied for or granted under any other provision of this Act. The court may, in granting legal aid under this section, specify one or more practitioners and the Director shall allocate the person requiring legal representation concerned to the practitioner so specified. Where a legal aid certificate is granted under this section, the court may order the legally aided person to contribute to the cost of the legal aid afforded to that person to an extent that the court considers just and r

A court may at any time grant legal aid to a person who is a party in any civil proceedings, whether at first instance or on appeal, in which the State is also a party if the court considers that— the person satisfies the conditions under which legal aid may be granted to that person under this Act; and it is in the interests of justice that the person be represented by a practitioner , other than the Director .
The powers of a court under subsection (1) shall be exercisable whether or not legal aid is applied for or granted under any other provision of this Act.

The court may, in granting legal aid under this section, specify one or more practitioners and the Director shall allocate the person requiring legal representation concerned to the practitioner so specified.

Where a legal aid certificate is granted under this section, the court may order the legally aided person to contribute to the cost of the legal aid afforded to that person to an extent that the court considers just and reasonable having regard to that person’s means and the maximum contribution to legal aid payable.

A court shall, on the grant of legal aid in a civil matter under this Act, notify the Director . The Director shall, on receipt of the notice under subsection (1), assign to the person granted legal aid for the purposes of the civil proceedings— a Legal Aid Counsel or Legal Aid Assistant; or a registered practitioner . A Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistant or practitioner shall, on receipt of a notice of the assignment from the Director , undertake legal representation of the person to whom a legal aid certificate is granted.
A court shall, on the grant of legal aid in a civil matter under this Act, notify the Director .

The Director shall, on receipt of the notice under subsection (1), assign to the person granted legal aid for the purposes of the civil proceedings— a Legal Aid Counsel or Legal Aid Assistant; or a registered practitioner .

A Legal Aid Counsel, Legal Aid Assistant or practitioner shall, on receipt of a notice of the assignment from the Director , undertake legal representation of the person to whom a legal aid certificate is granted.

Despite anything to the contrary contained in this Act, a person may refuse legal aid granted under this Part. Where a person refuses legal aid under subsection (1), the court shall— cancel the grant of legal aid to that person; permit that person to conduct the case in person or by a practitioner of that person’s choice without legal representation which is arranged by the Director ; and record reasons put forward by that person for the refusal. A court shall, where in any proceedings commenced an accused person refuses legal aid or the court cancels the grant of legal aid to the accused person, continue with and conclude those proceedings without legal aid . A cancellation of grant of legal aid under tis section shall not affect the right of the Director to require a person to contribute to the cost of any legal aid that may have been provided before the cancellation.
Despite anything to the contrary contained in this Act, a person may refuse legal aid granted under this Part.

Where a person refuses legal aid under subsection (1), the court shall— cancel the grant of legal aid to that person; permit that person to conduct the case in person or by a practitioner of that person’s choice without legal representation which is arranged by the Director ; and record reasons put forward by that person for the refusal.

A court shall, where in any proceedings commenced an accused person refuses legal aid or the court cancels the grant of legal aid to the accused person, continue with and conclude those proceedings without legal aid .

A cancellation of grant of legal aid under tis section shall not affect the right of the Director to require a person to contribute to the cost of any legal aid that may have been provided before the cancellation.

The Director may require any person seeking legal aid from the Legal Aid Board to pay a consultation fee as prescribed. The Director may waive the whole or part of a consultation fee payable under subsection (1), where the Director is satisfied that the person is unable to pay that fee. Despite subsection (1), the Board may specify the types of services under legal aid that do not require payment of consultation fees.
The Director may require any person seeking legal aid from the Legal Aid Board to pay a consultation fee as prescribed.
The Director may waive the whole or part of a consultation fee payable under subsection (1), where the Director is satisfied that the person is unable to pay that fee.
Despite subsection (1), the Board may specify the types of services under legal aid that do not require payment of consultation fees.

The Director may, in granting legal aid to any person under this Act, require that person to contribute to the cost of that legal aid to an extent which seems to the Director to be just and reasonable having regard to that person’s means or otherwise as prescribed. The Director may waive the whole or part of a contribution payable under subsection (1) where the Director is satisfied that the person is unable to pay that contribution. The Board may determine different methods of calculating a contribution to the cost of legal aid for criminal and civil cases. Any contribution that remains unpaid by a person required to pay a contribution under subsection (1) shall be recoverable as a civil debt as if the sum is adjudged to be payable to the Board . Where damages, costs or any property is recovered on behalf of a legally aided person , the costs incurred by the Director on behalf of the legally aided person less any contribution made by that person under subsection (1) shall be deducted

The Director may, in granting legal aid to any person under this Act, require that person to contribute to the cost of that legal aid to an extent which seems to the Director to be just and reasonable having regard to that person’s means or otherwise as prescribed.
The Director may waive the whole or part of a contribution payable under subsection (1) where the Director is satisfied that the person is unable to pay that contribution.
The Board may determine different methods of calculating a contribution to the cost of legal aid for criminal and civil cases.
Any contribution that remains unpaid by a person required to pay a contribution under subsection (1) shall be recoverable as a civil debt as if the sum is adjudged to be payable to the Board .

Where damages, costs or any property is recovered on behalf of a legally aided person , the costs incurred by the Director on behalf of the legally aided person less any contribution made by that person under subsection (1) shall be deducted by the Legal Aid Board from the monies relating to damages, costs or the property so recovered.

Where the court or the Director grants legal aid under this Act, and the Director makes a contribution order under subsection (1), the Director may direct that the grant of legal aid shall not take effect before the contribution is made.

Where a legally aided person fails to pay any contribution under this section when it is due, the Director may, cancel the grant of legal aid where the Director is satisfied that the person— was able to pay the legal aid contribution when it was due; and has failed or refused to do so.
The Director shall not cancel a grant of legal aid under subsection (7), without according a legally aided person an opportunity to be heard on the matter.

The Director , in ascertaining the means of any person for the purpose of this Act, shall take into account— that person’s income; and the personal and real property of that person, except that person’s dwelling house, the household, furniture the tools or implements of that person’s trade. The subject-matter of any dispute in relation to which legal aid is granted shall not be taken into account in assessing a person’s means. The Director shall, in assessing a person’s means, determine a person’s commitments for purposes of deductions of resources which would otherwise be that person’s means.

The Director , in ascertaining the means of any person for the purpose of this Act, shall take into account— that person’s income; and the personal and real property of that person, except that person’s dwelling house, the household, furniture the tools or implements of that person’s trade.
The subject-matter of any dispute in relation to which legal aid is granted shall not be taken into account in assessing a person’s means.
The Director shall, in assessing a person’s means, determine a person’s commitments for purposes of deductions of resources which would otherwise be that person’s means.

Where a court in respect of any proceedings before the court awards costs to a legally aided person , the costs should be paid to the Legal Aid Board and only the Director shall be capable of giving good discharge of the costs so payable. The Director may, for the purpose of receiving any costs payable to the Director under subsection (1), take steps and pursue remedies that could have been taken or pursued by a legally aided person to whom costs were awarded and the costs of taking those steps or pursuing those remedies may be recovered and received by the Legal Aid Board . Costs paid to the Legal Aid Board in pursuance of this section may be paid into and shall form part of the Fund , except that a legally aided person may be paid out of the costs paid to the Legal Aid Board the amount, or a portion of the amount that the Director considers just and equitable, of any contribution made by the legally aided person under section 51 . Subject to this section, the costs awarded by a court

Where a court in respect of any proceedings before the court awards costs to a legally aided person , the costs should be paid to the Legal Aid Board and only the Director shall be capable of giving good discharge of the costs so payable.

The Director may, for the purpose of receiving any costs payable to the Director under subsection (1), take steps and pursue remedies that could have been taken or pursued by a legally aided person to whom costs were awarded and the costs of taking those steps or pursuing those remedies may be recovered and received by the Legal Aid Board .

Costs paid to the Legal Aid Board in pursuance of this section may be paid into and shall form part of the Fund , except that a legally aided person may be paid out of the costs paid to the Legal Aid Board the amount, or a portion of the amount that the Director considers just and equitable, of any contribution made by the legally aided person under section 51 .

Subject to this section, the costs awarded by a court to a legally aided person shall be the costs which would have been payable if the services performed under legal aid had been performed by a practitioner in private practice on the instruction of a client without benefit of legal aid , and taxed accordingly.

The Director shall deduct an amount as determined by the Board from— damages or compensation awarded to a legally aided person by court ; and an amount payable to a legally aided person under settlement. Deductions paid to the Legal Aid Board under this section may be paid into and shall form part of the Fund .

The Director shall deduct an amount as determined by the Board from— damages or compensation awarded to a legally aided person by court ; and an amount payable to a legally aided person under settlement.
Deductions paid to the Legal Aid Board under this section may be paid into and shall form part of the Fund .

Where a court awards costs against a legally aided person , those costs shall not exceed the contribution which that person was required to make or which the court considers that person could reasonably have been required to make under section 51 , whichever is the greater. Costs awarded by a court against a legally aided person shall be paid out of moneys from the Fund .

Where a court awards costs against a legally aided person , those costs shall not exceed the contribution which that person was required to make or which the court considers that person could reasonably have been required to make under section 51 , whichever is the greater.
Costs awarded by a court against a legally aided person shall be paid out of moneys from the Fund .

The Director shall establish and maintain a register in the prescribed manner and form of— persons granted legal aid under this Act; registered practitioners, legal assistants, paralegals and legal aid service providers under this Act; and any other matter as may be prescribed. The registers shall be kept in the custody of the Director at the offices of the Legal Aid Board and shall be open for inspection to members of the public during normal office hours. The Director shall, on an application by any person, issue to the person a certified extract from a register or a copy of a certificate of registration on payment of a fee as prescribed.

The Director shall establish and maintain a register in the prescribed manner and form of— persons granted legal aid under this Act; registered practitioners, legal assistants, paralegals and legal aid service providers under this Act; and any other matter as may be prescribed.
The registers shall be kept in the custody of the Director at the offices of the Legal Aid Board and shall be open for inspection to members of the public during normal office hours.
The Director shall, on an application by any person, issue to the person a certified extract from a register or a copy of a certificate of registration on payment of a fee as prescribed.

A person who is aggrieved with a decision of the Legal Aid Board , may appeal to the Minister within thirty days of receipt of the decision. A person who is aggrieved with a decision of the Minister on appeal may appeal to the High Court.
A person who is aggrieved with a decision of the Legal Aid Board , may appeal to the Minister within thirty days of receipt of the decision.
A person who is aggrieved with a decision of the Minister on appeal may appeal to the High Court.

A Judge, judicial officer , correctional officer, police officer, prosecutor or any law enforcement officer shall inform a person in a court , correctional centre, police station, police post or any other place of detention, of that person’s right to engage the services of a practitioner or to apply for legal aid as provided under this Act. Information required to be provided under subsection (1), on the right to apply for legal aid shall be provided to a suspect or accused person prior to any questioning or at the time of deprivation of liberty. A police officer, correctional officer or any other law enforcement officer shall facilitate and assist any detained person who requests legal services to apply to the Legal Aid Board for legal aid .

A Judge, judicial officer , correctional officer, police officer, prosecutor or any law enforcement officer shall inform a person in a court , correctional centre, police station, police post or any other place of detention, of that person’s right to engage the services of a practitioner or to apply for legal aid as provided under this Act.

Information required to be provided under subsection (1), on the right to apply for legal aid shall be provided to a suspect or accused person prior to any questioning or at the time of deprivation of liberty.
A police officer, correctional officer or any other law enforcement officer shall facilitate and assist any detained person who requests legal services to apply to the Legal Aid Board for legal aid .

A practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider shall not demand, request or receive legal fees from a legally aided person in relation to services rendered under this Act. A practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten thousand penalty units. A person who wilfully obstructs a person held in detention from accessing legal aid under this Act commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten thousand penalty units.
A practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider shall not demand, request or receive legal fees from a legally aided person in relation to services rendered under this Act.

A practitioner , legal assistant , paralegal or legal aid service provider who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten thousand penalty units.
A person who wilfully obstructs a person held in detention from accessing legal aid under this Act commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten thousand penalty units.

The Director may request the Administrator-General to apply to the court for a grant of administration for the limited purposes of bringing or defending proceedings where— legal aid has been granted under this Act for the purpose of bringing or defending proceedings for the benefit of the estate of a deceased person or for the purpose of bringing proceedings under the Fatal Accidents Acts, 1846 to 1908, of the Parliament of the United Kingdom for the benefit of any dependant of a deceased person; and it appears to the Director that there is no person able and willing to take probate or letters of administration, or to be appointed administrator under the provisions of section 36 of the Local Courts Act. [ Cap. 29 ] The Court may, on an application under subsection (1), where the court considers necessary appoint the Administrator-General to be an administrator for the limited purpose specified under that subsection. Subsection (1) shall not be limited by the provisions of section 32 of

The Director may request the Administrator-General to apply to the court for a grant of administration for the limited purposes of bringing or defending proceedings where— legal aid has been granted under this Act for the purpose of bringing or defending proceedings for the benefit of the estate of a deceased person or for the purpose of bringing proceedings under the Fatal Accidents Acts, 1846 to 1908, of the Parliament of the United Kingdom for the benefit of any dependant of a deceased person; and it appears to the Director that there is no person able and willing to take probate or letters of administration, or to be appointed administrator under the provisions of section 36 of the Local Courts Act. [ Cap. 29 ]

The Court may, on an application under subsection (1), where the court considers necessary appoint the Administrator-General to be an administrator for the limited purpose specified under that subsection.
Subsection (1) shall not be limited by the provisions of section 32 of the Administrator-General’s Act. [ Cap. 58 ]

This section shall not be deemed to— limit the power of the Administrator-General to apply for a grant of probate or letters of administration under any other written law; or render the Administrator-General personally liable in any way for any act done by the Administrator-General in pursuance of the Administrator-General’s duties as an administrator appointed under this section.

The Minister may, on the recommendation of the Legal Aid Board , by statutory instrument, make Regulations for the better carrying out of the provisions of this Act. Despite the generality of subsection (1), the Regulations may make provisions for— the form of any certificate, application and other document which may be required for the purposes of this Act; the manner in which the means of any person who may be eligible for legal aid shall be computed; the manner of payment of any consultation fee required to be made by a person seeking legal aid ; the manner of payment and recovery of any legal aid contribution required to be made by a person to whom legal aid is granted; the manner of payment and recovery of any deduction required from awards to a person to whom legal aid is granted; the manner of payment of any fees required under this Act; and reports and information required by the Director for the purposes of this Act to be supplied by public officers and other persons.
The Minister may, on the recommendation of the Legal Aid Board , by statutory instrument, make Regulations for the better carrying out of the provisions of this Act.

Despite the generality of subsection (1), the Regulations may make provisions for— the form of any certificate, application and other document which may be required for the purposes of this Act; the manner in which the means of any person who may be eligible for legal aid shall be computed; the manner of payment of any consultation fee required to be made by a person seeking legal aid ; the manner of payment and recovery of any legal aid contribution required to be made by a person to whom legal aid is granted; the manner of payment and recovery of any deduction required from awards to a person to whom legal aid is granted; the manner of payment of any fees required under this Act; and reports and information required by the Director for the purposes of this Act to be supplied by public officers and other persons.
